Vlad on how the Robinhood product differs from Lighter's own app

"For one thing, it has a unique front end access through Robinhood wallet. For many customers, if they already have the wallet app, they can use it seamlessly. If they have the Robinhood app, they can fund the wallet with USDG and start trading perps if they're in a jurisdiction that has it"

"It'll be a different mix of markets, settled in USDG stablecoin. We've already seen some of the markets folks are interested in trading are more stocks, some memes as well. So it'll be a different customer base, different mix of markets"

"One thing that's interesting on the back end is there's a high level of interoperability between Robinhood chain and Lighter. A market maker can quickly move capital around between the two Lighter domains. That creates a lot of advantages, because the market maker can participate on both in a much more efficient way than if they were trading on one exchange on Solana and another on Ethereum"

"All of these interop protocols, like a LayerZero, like a Chainlink CCIP, that's an oracle. They're just white labeled with a different name"

Niklas Kunkel, CEO of Chronicle Labs, on what people fail to recognize about bridges and interop

"Anytime you use cross chain communication, anytime you're bridging a token from one chain to another chain, these are all oracles, just white labeled with a different name, like cross chain or bridge"

"So it really comes down to the LayerZero oracle was compromised, and the DeFi protocol was insufficiently risk managed to deal with that"

Niklas Kunkel, CEO of Chronicle Labs, on the rate gap that could drive tokenized equities

"If you have Tesla stock and you want to go borrow against your Tesla stock, your broker is going to charge you very high single digits, low double digits. Versus in DeFi, if you're going to lend these things with an LTV of 30% or something, DeFi will offer you mid to high single digits on this stuff all day"

"If you look at what you get for lending USDC, you're getting 2.7%. You're literally getting less than T bills"

"It's just going to be this ability to get leverage against your equities at much cheaper rates than you can in TradFi with your broker"

tweets.text:
I have a bot running 24/7 buying and selling for 0 target profit just to waste IRS manpower and money.

I print and send the IRS all the documentation about my trades in paper form to stop them from automatizing the process with a computer program.

I mix blank pages, double print, ghost print or mark/write in them just in case they have a way to scan and use some sort of recognition software. That way they have to look at thousands upon thousands of pages one by one and look and random scribblings and drawn dicks while doing their job.

It's a whole pallet of papers with trades so I hire a guy to forklift that shit and send it just in the offchance that having to deal with a pallet is troublesome for them.

All the documents are numbered but I pack everything *completely out of order* (as in the lower numbers always happen to be at the bottom of the pallet) in case the order of the trades is important. If order is important they now have to deal lift boxes and have them laying around before they can even start checking the numbers.

My normal trades are also in those pages so they can't just ignore everything.

This costs me thousands of dollars every year. I make sure to send the invoices of how much all the processes cost me on top of the pallet so that whoever is in charge of it knows I'm wasting the equivalent of two months of his waging in asinine shit on top of his or her time.

tweets.text:
Fascinating to hear Jeremy talk about how Silicon Valley underrates the thinkers and memetic ideas that drive it. 

From Curtis Yarvin to Will MacAskill, and how these worldviews are inside the models:

"There is a real philosophy, some sort of neo-Buddhist utilitarianism, that underlies the technological developments in Silicon Valley.

You see it, Will MacAskill gets involved with SBF and FTX. There's these thinkers like Nick Land. 

Their ideas percolate underneath the surface in the Valley and are influential in everyone, but are not necessarily named.

I thought it was remarkable for years how you could hear Curtis' ideas coming out of the mouths of the big tech leaders without being named.

In the Valley, in the development of these technologies, these cultural and philosophical ideas are underrated.

The models are highly utilitarian. They have this weird mix of religious ideas from Judaism and Buddhism, and this sort of utilitarian bent that turns into effective altruism. 

This whole cultural epicenter has been highly underrated in general in informing, why these things get built and what the worldviews inherent in them are. 

And what the inherent worldviews are in the people who build them. These things matter."

thread_tweets.text:
Following so much FUD on X, I asked Claude Opus 4.8 for WHY this law was being pushed so much:

"Everyone assumes Chat Control is Big Tech wanting your data. Meta already has your data, it doesn't need a CSAM law to read your Instagram DMs, so that framing is emotionally satisfying and mostly wrong.

The actual money trail is narrower. Thorn, the anti-abuse "charity" that lobbies hardest for these laws, also sells the scanning software, including to US Homeland Security, which means it's lobbying to create demand for its own product. The Oak Foundation has put over $24m into pro-scanning advocacy since 2019, and Johansson's own directorate funded WeProtect while WeProtect campaigned for her regulation.

But the real driver isn't a company at all, it's the interior ministries. Law enforcement has wanted a lawful way into private and encrypted comms for twenty years, and CSAM is the perfect wedge because no politician can vote against "protect the children" without the headline writing itself. Build the scanning pipe for that and you've built general-purpose surveillance infra that gets pointed at terrorism next, then whatever's convenient after.

The tell is which measures get dropped when the encryption pushback lands. A centre to remove known material at the source, safety-by-design on apps, the stuff that protects kids without scanning everyone, keeps stalling. The mass-scanning part keeps getting forced through on procedural tricks. When the parts that help children without surveillance are the negotiable ones, that ordering tells you what the priority actually is."

Oh man sounds bad:

By grok:

•Larry0x’s claim is technically correct: E2EE protects messages in transit and on servers, but the receiving (or sending) client app decrypts content locally to display it, allowing the software itself to scan plaintext if the law or app update requires it via client-side scanning.
•Current EU measure (July 2026 vote): The fast-tracked extension reinstates voluntary scanning for CSAM in unencrypted services like email, Instagram DMs, and iCloud, but does not mandate scanning or break E2EE in apps like WhatsApp, Signal, or Telegram.
•Broader context: While mandatory client-side scanning for E2EE was removed from recent Council positions to protect encryption, ongoing permanent CSA Regulation talks and “voluntary” pressure could still push on-device scanning in future, validating the post’s caution about E2EE’s limits against client mandates.

tweets.text:
It will extend to LLM's obviously, that's how it works. 

Venice is not "wrapping openrouter". Some models are sourced from there, but most are not.

tweets.text:
Bitcoin and gold sometimes move together, but they're not the same trade.

When people call Bitcoin "digital gold," they are basically saying that BTC is a scarce asset you buy when you're worried about inflation.

BUT, and this is a big but, the chart tells a different story.

Yellow = BTC price
Blue = gold price
Green = BTC/gold correlation

That green line shows whether both assets are actually moving together. When it spikes, they're aligned. When it drops, they're either diverging or moving opposite.

Right now, the green line keeps jumping around instead of holding steady.

So to me, even if BTC and gold share the same narrative, the market does not treat them like identical assets.

And if you hold both, you're not doubling down on one trade. IMO, you're holding two assets that sometimes overlap but can behave very differently when the market moves.

tweets.text:
Aerodrome is changing how it pays for liquidity.

Emissions are paid in AERO while fees come in as dollars, so when AERO rises, the protocol gets less profitable if fees don't outpace the price. Every week it mints new tokens that are paid to liquidity providers, which is a real cost to the business.

Aerodrome aims to fix this with the AER Engine, which pegs emissions to revenue instead of the token price and caps inflation within a set band. Spend follows what the protocol earns rather than what the token does, so a rising AERO price no longer mechanically pushes earnings down.

It also changes the shape of the cost line. A fixed weekly mint held spending flat regardless of what the protocol brought in. Linking emissions to fees replaces that with a cost that scales with the business.

tweets.text:
The most overlooked corner of AI could be biology.

Capital spent the last three years chasing compute and robotics while AI for biology lagged behind. The 2021 biotech collapse hit longevity especially hard, and the sector never drew the enthusiasm the rest of AI did. That skepticism lingered even as record investment flowed into the space around it.

The technology has started to change that. The methods that cracked protein folding are now reaching cellular problems that were previously out of reach.

Companies like Altos Labs, NewLimit, and Life Biosciences have drawn major funding. The FDA doesn't recognize aging as a disease, so companies target specific age-related illnesses first. The real ambition is to treat aging itself.

10,000 Americans turn 65 every day, and Social Security's reserves are projected to deplete in the early 2030s. GLP-1s could be the start of a much larger longevity wave.

(Original content provided by @PonderingDurian at Delphi Ventures)

Capital spent the last three years chasing compute and robotics while AI for biology lagged behind. The 2021 biotech collapse hit longevity especially hard, and the sector never drew the enthusiasm the rest of AI did. That skepticism lingered even as record investment flowed into the space around it.

The technology has started to change that. The methods that cracked protein folding are now reaching cellular problems that were previously out of reach.

Companies like Altos Labs, NewLimit, and Life Biosciences have drawn major funding. The FDA doesn't recognize aging as a disease, so companies target specific age-related illnesses first. The real ambition is to treat aging itself.

10,000 Americans turn 65 every day, and Social Security's reserves are projected to deplete in the early 2030s. GLP-1s could be the start of a much larger longevity wave.

(Original content provided by @PonderingDurian at Delphi Ventures)

Read the full report for free here.
https://t.co/hZfctVieVQ

tweets.text:
There are ~$30B of tokenized assets onchain (excluding stablecoins).

But only ~$3.2B is actually being used in DeFi.

Most RWAs are stuck at the "tokenized asset" stage, but the real growth comes when they reach the "productive asset" stage.

The first wave brought safe, familiar assets onchain like Treasuries, money market funds, gold, and credit. That part worked.

The second wave is integration. Tokenized Treasuries become collateral, Money market tokens become liquidity, and Real-world yields become trading exposure.

It's already happening now. RWA deposits in DeFi tripled from $2.33B to $7.44B YoY, while overall DeFi TVL fell 15%.

The biggest RWA assets right now:

• $USYC
• $BUIDL
• $XAUT
• $PAXG
• $SyrupUSDC
• $USDY

@ethereum still leads in RWA activity, but @solana is becoming the home for tokenized stocks. June tokenized stock volume hit ~$3.4B, and Solana did 90%+ of it.

RWA perps are waking up too. June was their first  month ever. People are trading tokenized equities, oil, gold, and even fiat currencies now.

So the shift is clear. People aren't just parking money in tokenized T-bills anymore. They're trading RWAs, borrowing against them, and using them across DeFi.

Stablecoins were crypto's first real PMF because they made dollars move faster. RWAs are running the same play for stocks, treasuries, funds, commodities, and credit.

Private markets will come later.

I'm not sure which category breaks out first, but the boring corner of crypto is the one with the clearest growth right now.

If you're bullish on RWAs, how are you getting exposure?

tweets.text:
I often observe people making decisions if their odds of being right are greater than 50 percent. What they fail to see is how much better off they'd be if they raised their chances even more (you can almost always improve your odds of being right by doing things that will give you more information). The expected value gain from raising the probability of being right from 51 percent to 85 percent (i.e., by 34 percentage points) is seventeen times more than raising the odds of being right from 49 percent (which is probably wrong) to 51 percent (which is only a little more likely to be right). Think of the probability as a measure of how often you're likely to be wrong. Raising the probability of being right by 34 percentage points means that a third of your bets will switch from losses to wins. That's why it pays to stress-test your thinking, even when you're pretty sure you're right.
#principleoftheday

tweets.text:
Spending 10 years in TradFi & Crypto, I learned that
​
"Life is a Portfolio"
​
You allocate your time/conviction across assets the same way you allocate capital.
​
"The individual positions may underperform 90% of the time but the discipline to find that 10% is what compounds, turning your life around"
​
Each position serves a different purpose. Together they produce returns no single one could.
​
Investing in Onchain AI taught me to diversify into AI equities
​
But my journey in getting started with equities wasn’t really smooth (as expected)
​
> Cumbersome KYC process with new brokers
> Had to Offramp, moved capital to the bank, and wired it to the broker
> Need to move money around again if a broker doesn't have certain stock
> Tried 3 tokenized stocks platforms but faced 10-50% slippage, clunky UI/UX, & often times tradable on market hours only
​
Worst experience = when I swapped $2k into a quantum stock & ended up getting $1.1k
​
These challenges happened 6+ months ago, up until I found a platform (it recently surpassed $2B in cumulative vol) + another that made it possible for tokenized stocks to be used as collateral for perps (which makes delta-neutral strat, cross-equity leverage possible)
​
My Journey + Strategy + Thesis of Productive Stocks era below ↓

tweets.text:
I had a conversation with ChatGPT about @sama being shady as fuck. Every step of the way, it directed me to watering down my message, especially comments regarding @elonmusk. 

But I saved the transcript. And the quoted message below is what ChatGPT recommended. But here it is unfiltered. 

I have also pasted into the entire ChatGPT conversation to the replies:

Sam Altman is shady as fuck. 

A lot of people, including very well-respected AI commentators and observers, have noted that he should likely step down. 

What they typically fail to do is give concrete examples of why. 

So, what I want to do now is explain why. 

I just watched his recent interview with Julia from CNBC. And when you watch that interview, it is so clear what his game is. Everything, that man does is meant to evoke weakness. The way he sits, the way he hunches his shoulders, the way he does not open his eyes even and look at Julia as he answers her questions, it’s all meant to evoke weakness and timidness. 

This is done, and I have seen it done a million times. I started my career in sales with ADP. I saw a lot of guys go this route. And what it is, be as non-threatening as possible so that you put your counterparty in a sense of ease, or even dominance. But in reality, you're manipulating them all the way. 

And these were the type of guys that, quite honestly, were the first ones to screw over the company, cheat on their wives, go out to parties late at night on a Friday after work is done, and start fights. They were the definition of passive-aggressive people, and that is Sam Altman to a T. 

It's not necessarily about the words he responds with, it's the demeanor. 

There was a moment when they zoomed out, they showed them both sitting, and you saw Sam with his shoulders hunched over, his eyes looking down, almost afraid to look her in the face as he answered, as a scared schoolboy. 

This is not about the content. This is about the delivery. 

This is about how he is delivering his message. 

And what's happening is, and what I saw in sales, was this actually often worked very well, especially with women, but with other men, they often noticed it as a ploy. And this aggravated them. And I think this is one of the main reasons why Elon is so aggravated with Sam. I think it has worked well thus far, but I think it's starting to fall flat, and that's why I said at the beginning a lot of very strategic AI commentators think he should step down and let the CFO Sarah Friar is a take over because this routine is getting old. 

And people are wanting to essentially, you know, see the proof in the pudding. And they want to see some sort of revenues. And then they're seeing a bunch of sketchy-ass business like filing the S-1, talking about going public, pulling back because they can't get a trillion-dollar valuation. Essentially the market is losing confidence in this guy, and they're starting to see past the routine. 

There's one other thing I want to add. And that's what I call the deserted island test. 

There are often two types of men. There are the men that can function well in a highly regulated society where force is essentially reserved for the state. And they are able, because of that fact, to essentially deploy mechanisms that would not work on what I call the deserted island. What often infuriates people is when men deploy these effects, and I believe Sam Altman is a master at this. And at the same time, individuals—and I think Elon Musk fits into this—recognize it and say, “if only.” And that “if only” is, “if only you were on a deserted island.” 

And I think the reality is Sam Altman does well in a well-regulated world where the use of force is highly controlled, but that his mechanisms for manipulation would resoundingly fail on a deserted island where force was available to his counterparties. And I think one thing about Elon is he's someone who scores high on both society's metrics as well as the deserted island. He's a big guy, for example. I think Sam Altman scores high on society's metrics for power and wealth, but I think on a deserted island, the way he presents himself would fail miserably. 

And what guys like Elon want deep down is a guy like Sam on the deserted island, and they just don't get that in society. But I think ultimately society does reward people who score high on both scales—the society scale and the deserted island scale. And I think Sam just fails miserably on the desert island scale, and that's why, at the end of the day, I think he's gotta go.

If the rewrite had taken 3 engineers 6 months, that is already roughly $300k-$500k of engineering cost, before management overhead, recruiting, coordination, opportunity cost, and the fact that those engineers are not building new features during that time.

And in the Bun article, Jarred says the manual version would probably have taken 3 engineers with full context about a year. That is easily a seven-figure project once you fully load it.

The AI version cost ~$165k, took 11 days, ran around 64 Claude agents in parallel at peak, generated 6,500+ commits, produced a 1M+ line Rust diff, and got to 100% of the test suite passing across all platforms before merge.

But the even more important point is that the cost of doing nothing is not zero at all!

If you stay on the wrong stack, you keep paying forever. You pay in bugs, crashes, memory leaks, security issues, slower development, harder onboarding, more review burden, and engineers being afraid to touch parts of the codebase. Bun’s whole point was that Rust gives them better tools for stability.

That has a real dollar value.

A rewrite is not just “new code.” A successful rewrite can buy you years of velocity, stability, maintainability, and security. It can make future bugs cheaper, future features easier, and future engineers more productive.

So no, $165k is not expensive. It’s cheap. And the best part is that it’s getting cheaper overtime because the cost of intelligence is going to zero. 

Expensive is keeping a critical codebase on a stack that makes every future change harder.
